hypothesis: B1-gpt's active-subspace Gauss-Hermite quadrature estimator has a genuinely better final-layer MSE than the champion (7.995e-06 vs 8.505e-06, ~6%) but lost the paired gate purely on mean_effective_compute (+28%) despite near-identical raw flops_used (+0.4%). Diagnosis: 1,360 fragmented small matmul calls vs. the champion's 32 single full-batch calls drive a flopscope/wall-clock overhead penalty unrelated to real arithmetic cost. Reimplementing the same statistical estimator with one combined (~6,516, width) batch forwarded through each layer with a single matmul call should recover most of that overhead while preserving the accuracy gain.

evaluation.command: uv run --frozen whest run --estimator <estimator> --runner subprocess --dataset hf://aicrowd/arc-whestbench-public-2026@v1-phase1 --split mini --flop-budget 272000000000 --format json

reports.note: Champion report reused byte-identical from the B4 experiment (same estimator.py@1598169, same deterministic dataset/seeds); candidate report freshly executed.

aggregate.reference_B1_gpt_note: candidate_final_layer_mse (7.995433086875892e-06) matches B1-gpt's original result (7.995440876130e-06) to 6 significant figures, confirming this is the mathematically identical estimator, just restructured for batched execution.

paired.comparison_to_B1: B1's paired_mean_delta was 1.966045905419e-07 (95% CI [-1.079e-07, 5.011e-07]); this run's mean_delta of 8.176e-08 is 58% smaller and the CI has narrowed and shifted toward zero, but is still not entirely below zero.

overhead_diagnostic.purpose: Quantify how much of B1's effective_compute penalty was recovered by batching, and how much remains.
overhead_diagnostic.conclusion: Batching the main 16-node quadrature sampling into one (~6,516, width) forward pass cut matmul call count from 1,360 to 353 (a 74% reduction) and the overhead ratio from 1.464 to 1.307 (about half the excess over the champion's 1.186 recovered). The remaining 321 extra calls (vs. champion's 32) come from two still-unbatched phases: 256 power-iteration matrix-vector products (4 iterations x 32 layers x 2 traversals) and 32 diagonal soft-gate propagation matmuls -- both currently done as per-layer vector ops rather than batched. Further batching those (e.g. running the power iteration as a small fixed-size batch, or algebraically folding the diagonal soft-gate pass into fewer calls) is the concrete remaining lever.

reason: The paired 95% CI [-2.268e-07, 3.903e-07] is not entirely below zero, so this does not clear the promotion gate, despite recovering roughly half of B1's excess effective-compute overhead (ratio 1.464 -> 1.307, still above the champion's 1.186) and cutting the relative regression from B1's +20.7% to +8.6%. The underlying accuracy gain is real and reproducible (final-layer MSE matches B1 to 6 significant figures) but is not yet large enough to overcome the remaining ~31,000 avg. extra matmul-call overhead vs. the champion.
